title: On-farm injection technique for livestock
subtitle: Safe subcutaneous and intramuscular injection across sheep, goats, and cattle.
excerpt: Giving a prescription injection on your own holding is the most time-critical skill in livestock first aid. Done correctly, it delivers the medicine cleanly, causes minimal stress, and leaves no injection site abscess. Done wrong, the needle bends, the medicine goes under the skin when it should be in the muscle, and the site becomes infected.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Sheep and Cattle Welfare Codes. SAC Consulting Scotland, Livestock Medicine Administration. Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013.

glossaryTerms.definition: An injection delivered directly into a muscle mass; the standard route for most antibiotic and anti-inflammatory products in livestock.
An injection delivered into the tissue directly under the skin; used for vaccines and some products that require slower absorption.

content.text: Injection site, needle gauge, needle length, and product route all vary by species and product. Getting the route wrong, giving a subcutaneous product intramuscularly or vice versa, changes absorption rate and sometimes causes tissue damage. Always read the product datasheet, and call your vet if any detail is unclear before injecting.
Sheep and goats: injection sites
For
intramuscular

injections: the neck muscle, behind and below the ear on the side of the neck, is the standard site. This muscle is large enough to take most volumes and easy to locate. Never inject into the hindquarters, sciatic nerve damage is a risk and the meat is wasted if the site abscesses.
For
subcutaneous

injections (vaccines, some wormers): the loose skin of the neck behind the ear, or the fold behind the shoulder blade in sheep. Tent the skin between two fingers and insert the needle at 45° into the tent. On withdrawal, gently rub the site to disperse the bolus.
Needle selection
Sheep and goats: 19 or 20 gauge, 25 mm length for IM; 20 gauge, 16 mm for SC
Lambs and kids: 21 gauge, 16 mm for both routes
Always use a new, sterile needle for each animal
Technique steps
Restrain the animal. Sheep: back against a corner, your knee across its neck. Goats: halter if necessary.
Draw up the correct dose. Check no air bubbles remain in the syringe.
Insert the needle quickly with confidence, hesitation and slow insertion hurts more than a single clean entry.
For IM: pull the plunger back slightly before injecting. If blood appears, withdraw and re-site, you're in a blood vessel.
Inject the full dose slowly. Withdraw cleanly. Record the treatment immediately in the medicine book.
Disposal
Needles go in a yellow sharps container, not in general waste. Most agricultural merchants supply sharps containers. Collect when full and dispose through your local pharmacy or veterinary practice.
